fields.name: Scotland
fields.description: Scottish mountains are stunning, rugged landscapes predominantly found in the Highlands, characterized by steep rocky ridges, deep glens, and lochs, often experiencing rapid weather shifts. They include 282 Munros (peaks > 3000ft) and form an ancient landscape shaped by glaciation. Major ranges include the steep North West Highlands and the rounded, plateau-like Grampians.

fields.name: Lake District
fields.description: The Lake District boasts England's ten highest mountains, offering dramatic landscapes of U-shaped valleys, sharp ridges, and rocky crags formed by ancient glaciation. These fells provide diverse hiking, from grassy slopes to steep, challenging ascents like Scafell Pike (978m), England's highest peak. The region is a central upland massif, often described as a radial wheel of high mountains, including iconic peaks like Helvellyn and Great Gable.

fields.name: Wales
fields.description: Welsh mountains are a dramatic landscape shaped by the Ice Age, characterized by rocky peaks, steep ridges, and glaciated valleys. They are divided into three main regions: the high, rugged peaks of Snowdonia (Eryri) in the north, the steep sandstone peaks of the Brecon Beacons (Bannau Brycheiniog) in the south, and the remote, rolling moorlands of the Cambrian Mountains in Mid-Wales

fields.name: Peak District
fields.description: The Peak District features rolling hills, dramatic gritstone edges, and limestone dales rather than true high mountains, with the highest point being the Kinder Scout plateau at 636 meters (2,087ft). The landscape is divided into the rugged northern "Dark Peak" (gritstone moorland) and the southern "White Peak" (limestone plateau), offering scenic vistas, steep valleys, and popular ridge walks.

fields.name: Yorskshire Dales
fields.description: The mountains of the Yorkshire Dales are characterized by steep, stepped gritstone edges, vast limestone pavements, and heather-covered moorland plateaus, with 41 peaks over 2,000 feet (Nuttalls). The landscape features iconic, distinctive shapes, most notably the Yorkshire Three Peaks—Whernside (736m), Ingleborough (723m), and Pen-y-ghent (694m)—which dominate the western skyline.
